coredata.dc:title: Oxygen reduction reactions from boron-doped graphene quantum dot catalyst electrodes in acidic and alkaline electrolytes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Hsieh, Chien-Te
Kao, Chih-Peng
Gandomi, Yasser Ashraf
Juang, Ruey-Shin
Chang, Jeng-Kuei
Zhang, Ren-Shuo
coredata.dc:description:
Background : Boron- and nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized and served as metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic and alkaline electrolytes.
Methods : The GQD catalysts were prepared using an efficient solvothermal technique based on the pyrolysis of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and boric acid at 180°C for 12 h. The as-prepared GQDs with an average particle size of ∼4 nm contain a high B/C and N/C level (B/C and N/C atomic ratio: 14.6 and 6.5 at.%) with a large amount of O-rich functionalities.
Significant Findings : The B- and N-codoped GQD catalytic electrodes provide ultra-high electrochemically active surface area for ORR with exceptional durability. Indeed, the ORR catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes is significantly enhanced via B- and N-doping into the GQDs lattices. Highly improved ORR activity was majorly due to the synergistic effect of the B- and N-codoped GQD catalysts, where the N functionalities facilitate the formation of C*–O2(ads) sites, whereas the B dopants regenerate the occupied and poisoned sites for the subsequent catalytic cycle. The solvothermal synthesis technique developed in this study sets the stage for developing high-performance metal-free catalytic nanomaterials for ORR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-free catalysts
Oxygen reduction reaction
Boron doping
Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials characterization of B-doped GQD catalysts
CV profiles of B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes
ORR activity of B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM micrographs of GQD and B-doped GQD samples. The inset shows their corresponding individual nanoparticles.
XPS (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, and (c) O 1s spectra of GQD sample, where each spectrum was deconvoluted by multiple Gaussian function.
XPS (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) B 1s spectra of B-doped GQD sample, where each spectrum was deconvoluted by multiple Gaussian function.
Top-view FE-SEM images of (a) pristine GQD and (b) B-doped GQD electrodes and pictures of B-doped GQD sample (c) before and (d) after boron mapping.
CV profiles of (a) GQD and (b) B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 under oxygen atmosphere.

LSV profiles of (a) GQD and (b) B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 under oxygen atmosphere. LSV profiles of (c) GQD and (d) B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes in 0.1 M KOH under oxygen atmosphere.
Current–time curves for ORR in (a) 1 M H2SO4 and (b) 0.1 M KOH for both catalyst electrodes.

head.ce:title: Oxygen reduction reactions from boron-doped graphene quantum dot catalyst electrodes in acidic and alkaline electrolytes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Materials Science and Engineering, National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 30010, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: : Boron- and nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized and served as metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acidic and alkaline electrolytes.

: The GQD catalysts were prepared using an efficient solvothermal technique based on the pyrolysis of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and boric acid at 180°C for 12 h. The as-prepared GQDs with an average particle size of ∼4 nm contain a high B/C and N/C level (B/C and N/C atomic ratio: 14.6 and 6.5 at.%) with a large amount of O-rich functionalities.

: The B- and N-codoped GQD catalytic electrodes provide ultra-high electrochemically active surface area for ORR with exceptional durability. Indeed, the ORR catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes is significantly enhanced via B- and N-doping into the GQDs lattices. Highly improved ORR activity was majorly due to the synergistic effect of the B- and N-codoped GQD catalysts, where the N functionalities facilitate the formation of C*–O2(ads) sites, whereas the B dopants regenerate the occupied and poisoned sites for the subsequent catalytic cycle. The solvothermal synthesis technique developed in this study sets the stage for developing high-performance metal-free catalytic nanomaterials for ORR.

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metallic nanoparticles (e.g., Pt supported on porous carbons) have been widely used as electrochemical catalysts within many electrochemical energy conversion/storage devices (e.g., fuel cells [1–3] and metal-air batteries [4,5]). Despite having very good performance, poor long-term stability, scarcity, and high cost are the major impediments preventing widespread adoption of precious metal catalysts [6–9] in such devices. Accordingly, recent attempts have been devoted for developing inexpensive non-precious metallic or metal-free catalysts [10–14]. More recently, heteroatom carbon nanostructures (e.g., N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [15]), have been explored as potential catalysts toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) due to their superior catalytic activity in acidic and basic electrolytes. It has been shown that the ORR catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes can be significantly enhanced via N-doping into the GQDs nanostructure [15]. The substitutional-N centers (within amidized carbon catalysts) greatly facilitate the adsorption of oxygen molecules onto the adjacent C atoms, breaking the O−O bond, and imparting the reduction of O2 via a four-electron reduction pathway [16].

Meanwhile, the N dopants at the edge (e.g., pyridinic N) or basal planes (e.g., graphitic N) of the amidized catalytic nanomaterials can potentially improve the electron-accepting capability through inducing a net positive charge on the adjacent carbon atoms and subsequently attracting the electrons liberated during the catalytic processes. This sequential process ultimately enhances the ORR activity [17], confirming the critical role of heteroatom doping on improving the catalytic activity in the carbon-based nanomaterials. Prior studies have shown functionalized GQDs prepared from different precursors [18–22] through effective synthesis methods. Moreover, doping GQDs with heteroatoms (e.g., O, N, B, S, etc.) is an effective strategy for modulating their intrinsic electronic, luminescence, and reactive properties [23–26]. It is generally recognized that N-doped graphene matrix can induce n-type conductivity [12]. On the contrary, boron doping can lead to p-type conductivity in the graphene structure due to lower electronegativity of boron atoms compared to carbon (i.e., 2.04 for B versus 2.55 for C) [12,13].

Given the promising role of nitrogen and boron doping on improving the catalytic activity of carbon-based nanomaterial, this study has been dedicated for incorporating B and N dopants into GQDs. To this end, a highly efficient solvothermal route was developed for synthesizing the B- and N-codoped graphene quantum dots. In addition, N-doped GQDs were also synthesized employing a similar solvothermal technique. The catalytic activities of as-prepared GQDs were analyzed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in acidic and alkali electrolytes. The findings of this study provide the essential framework for engineering B- and N-codoped GQDs as highly efficient and robust metal-free catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction.

The synthesis procedure of B- and N-codoped GQDs was described in our previous study [27]. Briefly, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) precursor was chemically modified through the nitration of pyrene in concentrated HNO3 [28,29]. The nitration process was moderately carried out, where the pyrene (2 g) was homogeneously mixed (by a magnetic bar at 150 rpm) in 240 mL HNO3 solution (16 N) at 80°C for 18 h. Afterward, the resulting mixture was cooled down to the ambient temperature and diluted with distilled water (2 L). The diluted solution was continuously stirred at room temperature for 24 h accelerating the attachment of the NOx groups to the TNP molecules. Finally, the mixture was filtered and dried at 60°C for 24 h to produce the TNP precursor.

The as-prepared TNP (2 g) powder and boric acid (1 g) was then dispersed in 50 mL dimethylformamide (boiling point: 153°C) using an ultrasonic bath at ambient temperature for 30 min. The homogeneous solution was placed in a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (120 mL). The solvothermal synthesis incorporated with in-situ B- and N-codoping process was performed at 180°C for 12 h and then cooled down to ambient temperature. The suspension containing organic-soluble B- and N-codoped GQDs were filtered through a microporous membrane (pore size: 0.02 μm) to remove any traces of insoluble impurities. Using a rotary evaporator, the solvent was removed and the final particles were recovered (at 45°C). To further elaborate on the influence of B dopant on the catalytic performance, the solvothermal method was also employed for synthesizing GQDs without any boric acid (i.e., pristine GQD samples). The B- and N-codoped GQD sample with the chemical ratios of boric acid to TNP at 1:2 in w/w was labeled as B-doped GQDs.

Employing X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS, Fison VG ESCA210), the chemical composition of the B-doped samples was characterized and the resulting spectra (C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s) were deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function. The structural morphology of the GQD-coated electrodes was observed with a field-emission scanning electron spectroscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-5600) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL, TEM-2100).

To prepare the catalytic electrodes, the GQD nanoparticles were deposited onto a commercially available carbon paper (CP, Toray Composites Inc.). The CP substrate used as the host structure for synthesizing the composite electrodes consisted of a series of carbon fibers arranged in the form of a porous and non-uniform configuration with the average fiber diameter of ∼8–10 μm. Followingly, 100 mg of the GQD powder was dispersed in a solution containing 30 mL of distilled water and 3 mL of Nafion 117 solution (Aldrich; 5 vol %). The GQD slurry was then placed in an ultrasonic bath for 0.5 h to ensure the formation of a uniform paste. A sequential dipping (layer-by-layer) method was adopted to deposit the GQDs onto CP substrate with a geometrical area of 2 × 1 cm2. Afterward, the GQD-coated CP electrodes were dehydrated at 80°C in a vacuum oven overnight.

To explore the catalytic activity of as-prepared electrodes, a typical three-electrode electrochemical cell was used. The GQD-coated CPs (as the working electrode), a Pt wire (as the counter electrode) and saturated Ag/AgCl (in 3 M KCl) reference electrodes were utilized within the setup and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out in 1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 KOH. The CV measurements were conducted under O2-saturated atmosphere at ambient temperature. The as-prepared catalytic electrodes were placed onto a stainless steel foil serving as the current collector and the CV profiles were recorded within the potential range of -0.2‒1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at various scan rates (10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s−1). To further inspect the ORR activity of GQD catalysts, a linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was also performed in 1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH with an O2 gas flowing at volumetric flow rate of 100 ml min−1. Priorly, high-purity oxygen was flowed throughout the three-electrode setup for ∼30 min, and all the LSV measurements were conducted at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials characterization of B-doped GQD catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: HR-TEM micrograph of pristine GQD sample, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), reveals a homogeneous dispersion of quasi-spherical nanoparticles. Based on Fig. 1(a), the as-prepared GQD sample possesses a relatively round shape with an average diameter of ∼4 nm. Several subgrains accompanying grain boundaries of individual GQD particle can also be observed, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(a). In each subgrain, a well-ordered lattice fringes with an average spacing distance of ca. 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite can also be identified, confirming the formation of a graphite-like structure [30]. As shown in Fig. 1(b), B-doped GQD sample also maintains a round-like morphology (average diameter of nanoparticles: ∼4 nm). The lattice fringes of B-doped GQD sample can be viewed along the (100) facet of graphite. This observation has also been confirmed in our previous studies [27,31].

XPS analysis was employed for examining the B-dopant concentration and distribution of surface functionalities induced by the introduction of boron dopants. Three main elements, C, N, and O, on the pristine GQD sample can be observed in different binding energies: 282‒288 eV, 399‒408 eV, and 531‒536 eV, respectively [32,33]. The N/C atomic ratio of the pristine GQD sample was assessed as ∼13.2 at.%. Considering the B-doped GQDs, an additional B peak at ca. 190‒194 eV can be detected [34], confirming the presence of B dopants within the graphitic grains. The B/C and N/C atomic ratios were 6.5 and 14.6 at.%, respectively. This finding indicates that the solvothermal synthesis method is capable of in-situ doping B and N atoms into the graphitic lattices, thus forming B- and N-codoped GQDs.

Fig. 3 shows high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, B 1s, and O 1s peaks of the B-doped GQD sample, deconvoluted by a Gaussian function. Compared to the pristine GQD sample, one additional C‒B group at ca. 283.9 eV [38,39] in the C 1s peak can be identified, indicating the presence of B dopants inserted into the graphite-like lattice. The N 1s spectrum was comprised of four components including N‒B bond (ca. 398.3 eV), pyridinic/pyrrolic N (ca. 399.6‒400.2 eV), graphitic N (ca. 401.4 eV), and NO2 (ca. 406.5 eV) [40,41]. It is important noting that the pyridinic/pyrrolic N has been identified to be bonded with the single-crystalline GQD lattice most probably at the edge sites rather than at the basal plane sites [27]. The B 1s spectrum of B-doped GQD sample includes three major peaks located at ∼189.2, 189.8, 190.6 and 191.9 eV, corresponding to B‒N, B‒C, BC2O, and BCO2 bands, respectively (see Fig. 3(c)) [42,43]. Therefore, it ca be concluded that the doping of B atoms into the skeleton structure of the GQD nanoparticles has been processed successfully.

FE-SEM images of pristine and B-doped GQDs electrodes are provided in Fig. 4 (a) and 4(b), confirming the presence of a large amount of GQDs deposited onto the surface of carbon fiber with an average diameter of ∼8 μm. The surface densities of B-doped and pristine GQDs were evaluated to be ∼0.30 mg cm−2, based on the increased electrode weight upon deposition of the GQD coating. Fig. 4(c) and 4(d) show the FE-SEM images before and after the B elemental mapping, respectively. According to Fig. 4, it is clear that the B atoms have been dispersed homogeneously throughout the GQD-coated electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV profiles of B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: As explained earlier, the CV experiments were conducted to examine the catalytic activity of different GQD catalytic electrodes in acidic (1 M H2SO4) and alkali (0.1 KOH) electrolytes under O2 atmosphere. Fig. 5 (a) and 5(b) depict the cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared composite electrodes at various scan rates of 10, 30, 50, and 100 mV s−1. As illustrated in Fig. 5, both GQD electrodes exhibit a well-defined redox pair, where there are one oxidation and reduction peak current at 0.48 and 0.28 V vs. Ag/AgCl on the B-doped GQD catalysts. With increasing the sweep rate, the peak currents are nearly proportional to the scanning rates, as shown in Fig. 5, indicating that the catalytic reaction toward ORR dominates the electrochemical reaction of B-doped GQD catalyst electrode in sulfuric acid. This result is similar to the other nanosized catalyst electrodes [44]. Comparing the pristine and B-doped GQDs, it is clear that the current density recorded with the B-doped GQD catalyst is much higher. At 100 mV s−1, the oxidation and reduction peak current of B-doped catalyst electrode reached as high as 2.55 and 2.51 A g−1, respectively (see Fig. 5). In contrast, the peak currents for reduction and oxidation on pristine GQD catalyst attain 0.38 and 0.92 A g−1, respectively. Therefore, the magnitude of redox current from B-doped catalyst is 6.7 (oxidation) and 2.7 (reduction) times higher than that of the pristine GQD sample. Indeed, the B-doped GQDs provide excessive active sites for the electrochemical adsorption/desorption of oxygen or proton in acidic electrolytes. Moreover, the ratio of oxidation to reduction peak current approaches to unity, confirming excellent reversibility on B-doped catalytic electrode.

One possible catalytic reaction pathway in acidic electrolytes under saturated oxygen environment can be formulated as [14,15,45],
(R1) C* + O2 ↔ C*−O2(ads)
(R2) C*−O2(ads) + 4 H+ + 4 e− ↔ C* + 2 H2O
(R3) C*−O2(ads) + 2 H+ + 2 e− ↔ C*−H2O2(ads)
(R4) C*−H2O2(ads) + 2 H+ + 2 e− ↔ C* + 2 H2O
where C* represents the active sites available for adsorbing oxygen molecules and C*–O2(ads) represents O2-adsorbed intermediates. The catalytic cycle on the GQD catalyst involves (i) chemsorption of oxygen molecules (R1), (ii) four-electron (R2), and (iii) two-electron charge transfer steps (R3 and R4). The imprved catalytic activity achieved with the B-doped GQDs can be attributed to one bi-functional reaction mechanism. On the surface of B- and N-codoped GQDs, the N sites are usually occupied by various oxygen-containing compounds, whereas the B sites facilitate stripping of the oxygenated species (e.g., −OH), due to the higher electro-negativity of N compared to B atoms (∼3.04 (N) vs. ∼2.04 (B)) [31]. In addition, B- and N-codoped GQD electrode contains a number of substitutional N atoms (e.g., pyrrolic and pyridinic N) at the edge of carbon network; providing numerous electroactive sites for adsorbing protons and storing H+ on the GQD structure. The density-functional theory (DFT) calculations have also shown that the substitutional N atoms improve the adsorption energy of hydrogen atoms compared to the neighboring C-atoms [45]. The amidized carbon surface (N/C atomic ratio: 14.6 at.%) accelerates the proton adsorption throughout the catalytic reaction pathway, followed by the charge-transfer (i.e., R2‒R4). This mechanism has also been reported for the improved catalytic activity using binary Pt-Sn [46] and Pt-Ru [47] catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR activity of B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: LSV measurements were conducted to investigate the ORR activity on the GQD electrodes using 1 M H2SO4 with the O2 gas flowing at 100 ml min−1, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). The maximal reduction currents of the pristine and B-doped GQD catalyst electrodes were 0.98 and 2.52 A g−1 at 100 mV s−1, respectively. The reduction current was significantly enhanced (∼2.6 times) after implementing the B doping within the GQD lattices. It is worth noting that the onset of ORR activity has the following order: pristine GQD (0.62 V) > B-doped GQD (0.51 V). It is generally recognized that the onset potential can be directly correlated to the catalytic activity [48,49]. Considering the onset potentials for ORR reveals that the boron doping enables enhanced electrocatalysis capability compared to the pristine GQD catalyst. Additionally, CO poisoning on the GQD catalysts can be vastly alleviated due to the presence of O-rich functionalities attached to the carbon nanostructure. Indeed, the hydroxyl groups (–OH) sites can effectively oxidize the CO into CO2 [50] mitigating commonly observed byproducts (due to poisoning of the catalyst) induced by the existence of CO within the Pt-based catalytic systems. It is well established that the pristine Pt catalyst exhibits relatively high ORR activity with ca. 4 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4 [46]. Although the ORR activity reported for the Pt catalyst is slightly higher than that of the B-doped catalytic electrodes demonstrated in this work; we believe that the ORR activity of B- and N-codoped GQD catalyst can be further improved through engineering the nanoparticles’ size distribution along with tuning the B/N/C ratio within the catalyst structure.

Fig. 6(c) and (d) present the LSV profiles on the GQD electrodes in 0.1 M KOH at different sweep rates with the O2 gas flowing at volumetric flow rate of 100 ml min−1. As clearly shown in Fig. 6, compared to the pristine GQD catalyst, B-doped GQD electrode delivers superior catalytic ORR activity in alkali electrolytes. At 100 mV s−1, the maximal reduction current of B-doped catalyst reaches as high as 6.0 A g−1, which is 2.7 times higher than that of the pristine one. The onset potential for B-doped catalyst appears at -0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl, which is more negative than that of the pristine electrode (∼0 V vs. Ag/AgCl). It has reported that the carbon atoms adjacent to the pyridinic N with Lewis basicity play a critical role serving as the active sites for adsorbing the oxygen molecules and subsequently initiating the ORR cycle [51]. This could be inferred from the reason why the ORR activity of B- and N-codoped catalytic electrodes in alkali electrolyte is better that that in acidic one, which is also observed in our previous study [15]. The higher ORR activity achieved with the B- and N-codoped catalytic electrodes is primarily due to excessive formation of C*–O2(ads) sites enabled by the N functionalities that accelerate the ORR reaction in the forward direction; whereas the B dopants refresh the occupied and poisoned sites for the next catalytic cycle [31]. Based on the DFT calculations, BC3 group can effectively alter the electronic transition, whereas the influence of BC2O and BCO2 (i.e., oxidized B bonding configurations) on the electronic properties of GQDs mostly depends on their hybridization form of the carbon [52]. Thus, the B-containing centers tend to localize the distribution of molecular orbitals, subsequently weakening the C−O bonding. Meanwhile, the pyridinic N doping at the edges formed through the introduction of unpaired electrons can also weaken the O−O bonding [53–55]. Additionally, stripping the oxygenated species of the B atoms can easily occur due to the difference in the electro-negativity between the B and O atoms; thus, favoring the subsequent ORR catalytic cycles [56].

To confirm the long-term catalytic activity and durability towards ORR, a chronoamperometry test at constant potential of 0.3 (for H2SO4 electrolyte) and -0.8 V (KOH) vs. Ag/AgCl was conducted for a period of 2 h. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the chronoamperometry profiles for both catalyst electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M KOH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7, the magnitude of the current density decreases (in both cases) within the initial stages due to the formation of intermediate species, e.g., C*–O2(ads) and C*−H2O2(ads) during the ORR process. After about 20 min, the current density gradually increases and stabilizes at a steady-state level. The stable current of B- and N-codoped GQD electrode is found to be 4.2 and 1.8 times higher than the pristine electrodes in acidic and alkali electrolytes, respectively. This trend is consistent with the insight gained through the CV analysis confirming the superior catalytic activity towards ORR achieved through the B- and N-codoped GQD catalytic electrodes.

In this work, a unique solvothermal technique was developed for synthesizing B- and N-codoped catalytic GQDs with high B/C and N/C atomic ratios (14.6 and 6.5 at.%, respectively). It was shown that the B- and N-codoped GQDs exhibit remarkable ORR activity in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes. In addition, significantly higher reduction currents were achieved after the B doping into the GQD lattices. The maximal reduction current of B- and N-codoped catalyst reached as high as 6.0 A g−1, which was 2.7 times higher than that of the pristine electrodes. The enhanced ORR activity of as-prepared codoped catalytic electrodes was further confirmed via conducting the linear sweep voltammetry experiments at various electrolyte configurations. The improved ORR activity mainly originated from the N functionalities accelerating the formation of C*–O2(ads) sites, while the B dopants refresh the occupied sites for the sequential catalytic cycles. Accordingly, the design of B- and N-codoped GQDs demonstrated in this work can be adopted for synthesizing non-metallic and inexpensive catalysts with superior durability, selectivity, and high catalytic activity toward ORR.

The authors are very grateful for the financial support the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3). Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2H0062 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization and single cell testing of Pt/C electrodes prepared by electrodeposition

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activity benchmarks and requirements for Pt, Pt-Alloy, and Non-Pt oxygen reduction catalysts for PEMFCs
